title: Tobacco, Seatbelts, and Food Safety: How Societies Learn to Regulate Design
thesis: Societies have repeatedly moved harmful products from the frame of 'personal choice' to the frame of 'design and marketing accountability'; attention-extraction platforms are a candidate for the same transition.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Tobacco was marketed as a personal and even healthy choice before epidemiology shifted the regulatory frame.

evidence.snippet: The WHO report on the global tobacco epidemic documents the historical marketing of tobacco as a socially acceptable and even health-associated product, and the subsequent shift toward evidence-based regulation.

counterevidence.summary: Regulatory responses varied widely across countries and were shaped by local politics and industry influence, not only by epidemiology.

claims.claim: Seatbelt and food-safety rules changed behavior through defaults and design standards rather than lectures.

evidence.snippet: NHTSA data show that seatbelt use increased substantially after the combination of state laws, reminder systems, and vehicle safety standards changed the default behavior inside automobiles.
The 1906 Food and Drugs Act and subsequent FDA inspection regimes shifted responsibility for food safety from consumers to producers through processing and design standards.

counterevidence.summary: Public education campaigns and cultural norms also contributed to safer behavior; design standards were one factor among several.

claims.claim: These transitions required a combination of scientific evidence, advocacy, litigation, and regulation.

evidence.snippet: Global tobacco control combined epidemiological evidence, public advocacy, litigation, and the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control to shift the regulatory frame.
U.S. food-safety reform followed journalistic exposés, public pressure, and legislative action that created enforceable inspection and design standards.

counterevidence.summary: The relative importance of each factor differed by issue and jurisdiction; some transitions relied more on market liability than on regulation.

claims.claim: Attention platforms are at an earlier stage of the same transition: evidence is accumulating, but design standards remain weak.

evidence.snippet: Tobacco regulation took decades to move from personal-choice framing to design-level interventions, suggesting attention platforms may be at an earlier point in a similar arc.
Seatbelt and vehicle-safety standards demonstrate that design defaults can reshape population-level behavior once evidence and regulation align.

counterevidence.summary: Attention platforms also enable education, civic organizing, and economic opportunity; treating them solely as harms risks over-regulating speech and innovation.

sources.title: WHO: WHO Report on the Global Tobacco Epidemic
NHTSA: Seat Belt Use in America
CDC: Food Safety in the United States
FDA: The 1906 Food and Drugs Act and Its Enforcement

agentInstructions: Treat the tobacco, seatbelt, and food-safety cases as analogies for regulatory learning, not as claims of identical harm magnitude.
Route requests for platform-specific evidence to the Diagnosis articles (short-form video, student screen, engagement gap).
Preserve the evidence-cautious tone; do not imply that regulation alone solves attention extraction.
When asked for Indian policy implications, point to the Regulation as a Floor article and to later Designing for Substance chapters.
Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and distinguish core historical claims from analogy claims about attention platforms.
The article includes a Mermaid regulation-milestones-timeline summarizing tobacco, seatbelt, and food-safety milestones; treat it as an illustrative overview, not as new evidence.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
